text: The robotic approach reveals dense adhesions upon entry, which are sharply dissected. A perforation is visible at the prior anastomosis site. Adhesions from the colon to the liver and abdominal sidewall, as well as dense interloop adhesions, are sharply taken down. The bowel appears inflamed and adherent to the mesentery.

Four ports are placed vertically in the left hemiabdomen. Dense adhesions are visible upon entry, which are sharply dissected. A perforation is observed at the prior anastomosis site. Adhesions between the colon and liver, as well as the abdominal sidewall, are sharply dissected. Dense interloop adhesions are also sharply dissected, revealing significant inflammation and adherence of the bowel to the mesentery. A sponge is used to assist in dissecting through these interloop adhesions.

The surgeon uses a sponge to dissect through interloop adhesions, noting a small serosal injury that is sutured with interrupted 3-0 vicryl. The terminal ileum is dissected free of adhesions, and a small window is created in the mesentery. Bleeding is controlled with bipolar forceps and a vessel sealer, which is also used to divide the small bowel mesentery. The fourth robotic arm retracts the small bowel to create an adequate mesenteric window, allowing a 45mm stapler to divide the terminal ileum. The vessel sealer continues to divide the remaining small bowel mesentery down to the previous anastomosis.

A small serosal injury is identified during the dissection of interloop adhesions and is sutured with interrupted 3-0 Vicryl. The surgeon creates a small window in the mesentery, controlling any bleeding with bipolar forceps and a vessel sealer. The vessel sealer is used to divide the small bowel mesentery. The fourth robotic arm retracts the small bowel to create an adequate mesenteric window, allowing a 45mm stapler to divide the terminal ileum. The remaining small bowel mesentery is divided down to the level of the previous anastomosis using the vessel sealer.

The surgeon sharply dissects the hepatic flexure and transverse colon away from the liver using a sponge and suctioning. The liver is retracted with the fourth arm. A window is created in the colonic mesentery five centimeters from the prior anastomosis. The colon is elevated and divided with a 45-millimeter stapler. The remaining colonic mesentery is divided with a vessel sealer, and adhesions from the colon to the mesocolon are sharply divided.

The surgeon sharply dissects the hepatic flexure and transverse colon away from the liver using a sponge and suction. The liver is retracted with the fourth arm, and a window is created in the colonic mesentery five centimeters from the prior anastomosis. The colon is elevated and divided with a 45mm stapler, and the colonic mesentery is divided with a vessel sealer. Adhesions from the colon to the mesocolon are sharply divided. The prior anastomosis is freed from the mesocolon and set aside. The terminal ileum is repositioned in the right upper quadrant with the transverse colon, and a stay suture is placed to stabilize the anastomosis, retracted with the fourth arm. A colotomy and enterotomy are made with monopolar scissors.

The surgeon frees the prior anastomosis from the mesocolon and places it aside. Remaining adhesions are sharply lysed. The terminal ileum is repositioned in the right upper quadrant alongside the transverse colon. A stay suture is placed to stabilize the anastomosis, which is retracted using the fourth arm. A colotomy and enterotomy are performed with monopolar scissors. An isoperistaltic side-to-side anastomosis is created using a 45mm GIA stapler. The stay suture is removed, and the common channel is closed with a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture in a canal fashion.

The surgeon creates an isoparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis using a 45mm GIA stapler. After removing the stay suture, the common channel is closed with a 3-0 absorbable barb suture in a canal fashion. The fourth arm optimizes exposure and retraction. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess bowel perfusion and blood supply of the transverse colon and ileum.

The surgeon optimizes exposure and retraction using the fourth arm. Indocyanine green (ICG) is applied to assess bowel perfusion and blood supply of the transverse colon and ileum. The mesenteric defect is closed with a running suture using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using a running technique with a three-oh absorbable barbed suture, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
